stem: Set \( A \) has [[m]] elements and set \( B \) has [[n]] elements. How many distinct functions are there from \( A \) to \( B \)?
solution: Each of the [[m]] elements of \( A \) may be sent independently to any of the [[n]] elements of \( B \), so there are \( [[n]]^{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \) functions.
distractor_rationales: This swaps the roles of the domain and codomain sizes.
This counts a combination as though order/repetition of images did not matter.
This is the number of one-to-one (injective) functions, not all functions.
This multiplies the two sizes instead of exponentiating.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Set \( A \) has [[m]] elements and set \( B \) has [[n]] elements. How many distinct one-to-one (injective) functions are there from \( A \) to \( B \)?
solution: The first element of \( A \) can map to any of [[n]] elements, the second to any of the remaining \( [[n]]-1 \), and so on for [[m]] elements: \( \frac{[[n]]!}{([[n]]-[[m]])!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts ALL functions, not just the injective ones (it allows repeated images).
This counts the images as an unordered set, losing the distinction between which element of \( A \) maps where.
This uses the wrong factorial in the denominator.
This only accounts for arranging the elements of \( A \) among themselves, ignoring the larger codomain.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Sets \( A \) and \( B \) each have [[n]] elements. How many bijections (functions that are both one-to-one and onto) are there from \( A \) to \( B \)?
solution: A bijection between two [[n]]-element sets is a full arrangement (permutation) of \( B \) assigned to the elements of \( A \): \( [[n]]! = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the number of ALL functions from \( A \) to \( B \) (allowing repeats), not just bijections.
This is the number of subsets of an \( n \)-element set, unrelated to counting bijections.
This forgets to place the last remaining element, stopping the factorial one step early.
This only accounts for placing the first two elements.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Set \( A \) has [[m]] elements and set \( B \) has [[n]] elements. How many distinct onto (surjective) functions are there from \( A \) to \( B \)?
solution: By inclusion-exclusion over the images missed, the number of onto functions is \( \sum_{i=0}^{[[n]]} (-1)^i \binom{[[n]]}{i} ([[n]]-i)^{[[m]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the count of ALL functions, without removing the ones that miss at least one element of \( B \).
This only counts bijections between equal-size sets, ignoring the actual sizes here.
This picks the wrong pair of sets to choose from.
This is the number of ways to distribute [[m]] IDENTICAL items into [[n]] distinguishable nonempty groups, not the number of onto functions between labeled sets.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Set \( A \) has [[m]] elements and set \( B \) has [[n]] elements. A relation from \( A \) to \( B \) is any subset of \( A \times B \). How many distinct relations are there from \( A \) to \( B \)?
solution: \( A \times B \) has \( [[m]]\cdot[[n]] \) ordered pairs, and a relation is any subset of them, so there are \( 2^{[[m]][[n]]} = [[answer]] \) relations.
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the number of subsets of \( A \) by the number of subsets of \( B \) instead of counting subsets of \( A\times B \).
This is just the size of \( A \times B \), not the number of subsets of it.
This is the number of functions from \( A \) to \( B \), a much smaller count than all relations.
This swaps the roles of \( A \) and \( B \) in the function-count formula.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Let \( A \) be a set with [[n]] elements. A (binary) relation on \( A \) is any subset of \( A \times A \). How many distinct relations are there on \( A \)?
solution: \( A \times A \) has \( [[n]]^2 \) ordered pairs, and a relation is any subset of them, so there are \( 2^{[[n]]^2} = [[answer]] \) relations on \( A \).
distractor_rationales: This is the number of subsets of \( A \) itself, not of \( A \times A \).
This is the number of functions from \( A \) to \( A \), a much smaller count than all relations.
This uses \( 2n \) instead of \( n^2 \) as the exponent, undercounting the pairs.
This is the number of permutations of \( A \), unrelated to counting relations.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Set \( A \) has [[m]] elements and set \( B \) has [[n]] elements. How many functions from \( A \) to \( B \) are NOT one-to-one (i.e., at least two elements of \( A \) share an image)?
solution: Start with all \( [[n]]^{[[m]]} \) functions and remove the \( \frac{[[n]]!}{([[n]]-[[m]])!} \) that are injective: \( [[n]]^{[[m]]} - \frac{[[n]]!}{([[n]]-[[m]])!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the count of ALL functions, forgetting to remove the injective ones.
This is the count of the injective functions themselves — the complement of what was asked.
This treats the images as an unordered selection rather than counting functions.
This swaps the roles of the domain and codomain sizes.
This is off by one from the correct total.
This is off by one from the correct total.
